focus: Git publication invariants and comparison of concurrent fast-forward integration strategies
noveltyJustification: All 6 findings are new in this initialized lineage; no prior iteration findings existed.
keyQuestions: Which integration strategy best guarantees continuous fast-forward publication under concurrent writers: rebase/retry, serialization, merge queue, or scratch-index/ref-level commit construction?
answeredQuestions: Which integration strategy best guarantees continuous fast-forward publication under concurrent writers: rebase/retry, serialization, merge queue, or scratch-index/ref-level commit construction?
ruledOut: Uncoordinated direct pushes as a zero-rejection guarantee
A hosted merge queue as the sole low-latency continuous autosync mechanism

text: A branch update is fast-forward only when the new tip descends from the current remote tip; two writers based on one tip cannot both push divergent descendants without one first integrating the other.
Fetch/rebase/retry is an optimistic retry protocol, not a bounded-progress guarantee; a changed remote tip causes rejection and overlapping changes may require conflict resolution.
A single publisher is the strongest default for a continuously linear shared branch, provided all writers route through it and it still verifies the observed remote tip before publication.
inference: Git fast-forward rejection plus old-oid conditional ref updates imply that eliminating writer races requires a serialization point, not merely more retries.

text: A temporary index can build a tree and commit without changing a session worktree, and update-ref can install the result conditionally on the expected old tip; this isolates commit construction but is only a local guarantee.
GitHub merge queues serialize validated merge groups against the latest base, but conflicts or failed checks remove entries, so they do not guarantee continuous blocker-free publication.

Zero operator-visible divergence is an orchestration property that can hide retries; it cannot mean automatic preservation of arbitrary incompatible overlapping intent without an explicit conflict policy.
inference: Git requires a combined descendant before a second divergent history can fast-forward, while GitHub removes merge-queue entries that conflict with the base.

reason: The first accepted divergent descendant makes the second a non-fast-forward update.

direction: Force push on the shared branch
reason: It disables the history-preservation check and can discard concurrent commits.

direction: Hosted merge queue as sole autosync engine
reason: It is PR/check-oriented and explicitly removes conflicts or failed groups instead of guaranteeing publication.
